icd_category: J09.X2 — Influenza due to identified novel influenza A virus with other respiratory manifestations
one_liner: A 55-year-old male with COPD presents during flu season with acute-onset high fever, severe myalgias, dry cough, and prostration beginning 2 days ago.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient was in his usual state of health until 2 days ago when he developed sudden onset of high fever (40°C), severe diffuse myalgias, frontal headache, non-productive cough, rhinorrhea, and profound fatigue. He reports feeling 'hit by a truck' and has been unable to get out of bed. He has mild sore throat and retrosternal chest tightness with coughing. He did not receive the influenza vaccine this season. His wife had a similar illness last week. He has COPD and uses inhalers but has not required hospitalization for exacerbations. He denies hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or shortness of breath at rest. No recent travel.
stage2.pmh: COPD (moderate, GOLD stage II), hypertension, former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 5 years ago).
stage2.medications: Tiotropium 18 mcg inhaled daily, albuterol inhaler PRN, lisinopril 10 mg daily.
stage2.social_history: Retired plumber. Former smoker (quit 5 years ago). Occasional alcohol. No illicit drugs. Lives with wife. Did not receive influenza vaccination this year.
stage2.physical_exam: Ill-appearing, flushed male in mild respiratory discomfort. Oropharynx mildly erythematous without exudates. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Scattered bilateral expiratory wheezes, no crackles. Heart tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen benign. No rash. Mild conjunctival injection bilaterally. Diffuse muscle tenderness on palpation.

lab_results.clinical_significance: confirms the suspected diagnosis infection; RT-PCR is the gold standard with >95% sensitivity
Normal to low-normal WBC; leukopenia or relative lymphopenia is common in influenza
Moderate elevation consistent with viral infection; markedly elevated CRP would suggest bacterial superinfection
Mildly elevated AST can be seen with influenza; no significant hepatic involvement

imaging_results.result: Hyperinflated lungs consistent with COPD. No focal consolidation, effusion, or infiltrates. No pneumothorax.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: No evidence of pneumonia; findings consistent with underlying COPD only

key_tests: Rapid influenza molecular assay (RT-PCR) — preferred test for influenza diagnosis
low_yield_tests: Rapid antigen influenza test (lower sensitivity than PCR; false negatives common)
Viral culture (too slow for clinical decision-making)

stage5.final_clue: Classic influenza presentation during flu season: abrupt onset of high fever, severe myalgias, headache, dry cough, and prostration in an unvaccinated patient with a sick household contact. this condition (H3N2) confirmed by RT-PCR. Underlying COPD with mild hypoxia warrants close monitoring.

debrief.classic_presentation: Abrupt onset of high fever (often >39°C), severe myalgias, headache, malaise, and non-productive cough during influenza season (typically October-April in the Northern Hemisphere). Distinguished from the common cold by sudden onset, systemic symptoms predominating over upper respiratory symptoms, and prostration. Patients often describe being 'hit by a truck.' Conjunctival injection and facial flushing are classic but often overlooked findings.
debrief.pathophysiology: Influenza A and B viruses infect respiratory epithelial cells via hemagglutinin binding to sialic acid receptors. The virus replicates in the respiratory epithelium, causing direct cytopathic damage and triggering an intense innate immune response with release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma), which account for the systemic symptoms (fever, myalgias, malaise). Antigenic drift (point mutations in hemagglutinin and neuraminidase) allows seasonal re-infection. Antigenic shift (reassortment of genome segments between different influenza A strains) can produce pandemic strains. Complications include primary viral pneumonia, secondary bacterial pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, H. influenzae), COPD/asthma exacerbations, myocarditis, and encephalitis.
debrief.treatment_overview: Antiviral therapy with oseltamivir (Tamiflu) 75 mg PO BID for 5 days, ideally started within 48 hours of symptom onset. Benefits are greatest when started early but can still provide benefit in hospitalized patients even if started >48 hours. Alternative: baloxavir marboxil (Xofluza) 40-80 mg single dose. For hospitalized or severe cases, oseltamivir is preferred (more clinical data). Supportive care: antipyretics (acetaminophen/ibuprofen), hydration, rest. For this patient: optimize COPD management (increase bronchodilator use, consider short course of systemic corticosteroids if wheezing worsens), supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >92%, and monitor for secondary bacterial pneumonia. Hospitalization criteria: hypoxia, respiratory distress, altered mental status, high-risk comorbidities with moderate-severe disease.
high_yield_pearls: RT-PCR (molecular assay) is the preferred diagnostic test for influenza — rapid antigen tests have sensitivity of only 50-70% and should not be used to rule out influenza.
Oseltamivir should be started empirically in hospitalized patients with suspected influenza during flu season without waiting for test results.
High-risk groups for influenza complications: age >65, COPD/asthma, cardiac disease, diabetes, immunosuppression, pregnancy, obesity (BMI >40), and nursing home residents.
A low procalcitonin (<0.25 ng/mL) in influenza can help distinguish primary viral illness from bacterial superinfection and guide antibiotic stewardship.
Secondary bacterial pneumonia typically occurs 4-7 days after influenza onset, often heralded by initial improvement followed by recurrent fever and productive cough.
sources: IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-Acquired Pneumonia (2019)

Uyeki TM et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines by the IDSA: 2018 Update on Diagnosis, Treatment, Chemoprophylaxis, and Institutional Outbreak Management of Seasonal Influenza. Clin Infect Dis. 2019;68(6):e1-e47.
UpToDate: Seasonal influenza in adults — Clinical manifestations and diagnosis (2024)

after_stage2_more_likely: Influenza — abrupt onset of high fever, severe myalgias, dry cough during flu season in unvaccinated patient with sick household contact; classic presentation
COVID-19 — overlapping symptoms (fever, cough, myalgias) but COVID more often has anosmia/ageusia and more gradual onset
after_stage2_less_likely: Community-acquired pneumonia — no focal lung findings on exam; predominantly systemic rather than pulmonary symptoms
Acute bronchitis — typically lower fever, less systemic toxicity, and not this acute in onset
after_labs_top3: Influenza A — confirmed by RT-PCR (H3N2); low procalcitonin and clear CXR exclude bacterial superinfection
COVID-19 — excluded by specific influenza A detection on molecular testing
Bacterial pneumonia — excluded by clear CXR, low procalcitonin, and normal WBC
